content:
14 min read
Just now

--

You know that moment in every game tutorial where you finally get a little guy walking around a field, and you feel like a wizard for about nine seconds, and then you close the tab and he’s gone? Vaporised. No memory of you. Rude.
We’re not doing that. By the end of this post you’ll walk a courier around a small map, close the browser, go live your life, come back tomorrow, and find her standing in the exact spot where you abandoned her. Slightly judgemental, but standing there.
Cloud functionality is normally where tutorials tap out, because it means a server, a database, a login form, and a weekend (or six) you are never getting back. We’re going to do it in about ten lines.
What you’ll have by the end of the whole series? A fully polished, beautiful top-down RPG called Courier of the Last Shrine: three maps, some enemies who want you dead, a boss, loot, a leaderboard, and async duels with your friends. AI generates the art, writes the story, and most of the code. You’re the director, the creative human touch, the one with taste and opinions. It’s 2026. That’s the job.
We’ll make it work first, then make it pretty, focusing on the functionality in the first set of tutorials, and the visuals and finishing touches when everything works flawlessly.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The three accounts you need
If you already have these, skip ahead, nothing to see. If you’re starting from nothing, this takes about ten minutes.
Claude Code — or another AI coding agent
I’m using Claude Code, because it’s just too good not to, but Cursor or anything similar works fine, the prompts in this series aren't Claude-specific.
Install it macOS, Linux, and WSL with this:
curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bashOr on Windows PowerShell with
irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iexThen cd into your project folder, run claude, and log in through the browser prompt. One thing that catches people: Claude Code needs a Pro, Max, Team, Enterprise, or Console account. The free Claude.ai plan doesn’t include it. Pro is the usual starting point for this kind of thing.
Triggair — for cloud functionality
Create an account on Triggair (the free tier should work fine for this tutorial).
The free tier gives you one game, five monthly active players, and the whole core backend: saves, leaderboards, economy, cheat protection, all of it. Five players sounds tiny until you remember that’s you and four friends playing your weird little courier game, which is genuinely the entire goal here. Everything in this series runs on it. If your game takes off and you need more than five humans, that’s a lovely problem and the Dev tier is still just $5.
Runflow — for the art
Runflow is like kind of like fal.ai, but I find it more convenient to integrate to my coding agent’s pipeline. For the images we are going to use Google’s Nano Banana 2, if you arelady have a way to use it, maybe skip this part. You get some free credits for a new Runflow account.
For scale: Nano Banana 2 runs $0.08 per image at 1K. All the art in this post, including the tileset attempt I fumbled and had to redo, came to about fifty cents. The coffee(s) I drank during writing this article cost an order of magnitude more.
First, feed your agent the docs
I’m putting this first because it’s the most important thirty seconds in the entire series and everyone skips it.
Coding agents are great at Phaser. Fifteen years of Phaser questions exist on the internet and it has read all of them, probably twice. But when it doesn’t know something, it does the thing we all did in exams: writes something that looks right and hopes for the best. That’s how you get endless nights of debugging and then manually fixing the code. We don’t want that.
Hand it the reading material.
Let’s start with Runflow, create an API key under Settings / API Key, and note it down.
Then head to the model page, each has an LLMs link in the top righ corner.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Copy the link and write the instructions:
Read https://app.runflow.io/models/google/nano-banana-2/llms.txt to
understand how to invoke Nano Banana 2 using their API. When invoking
the Runflow API, always use this key: ***Cool, now let’s do the same wit Triggair. After login, you will see a button to generate an access token, it conveniently injects it into the prompt.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Click the Copy prompt button and paste the results to your agent.
Integrate Triggair, a complete backend for browser games, into my game.First read the integration guide: https://triggair.com/skill.md
(concise index: https://triggair.com/llms.txt, full API reference:
https://triggair.com/docs/api)
It provides identity, cloud saves, leaderboards, an economy, live-ops,
moderation, and realtime behind one import (@triggair/sdk) and one
publishable key. Wire the features my game needs by following that guide.
To create the game and configure its server-side definitions
(leaderboards, currencies, stores, loot, flags, quests, and so on), use
the management API at https://api.triggair.com/v1/dev/* or the MCP server
at POST https://api.triggair.com/v1/mcp, authenticated with this token:
Authorization: Bearer ***
Start by creating a game (POST /v1/dev/games) and issuing a publishable
key, then wire the SDK in my client with that key. The token has full
account access, so keep it out of client code.
If your agent does skills, drop https://triggair.com/skill.md in its skills folder and never think about this again.
At this point you can decide to create the game manually on the website, or just tell your AI agent to create a new game in Triggair called “Courier of the Last Shrine” and have the MCP integration do it’s magic.
Step 1: scaffold the thing
A simple prompt should do it:
Create a new browser game project in a folder called "courier":
- Vite with TypeScript, no framework
- phaser as a dependency, pinned to 4.2.1
- @triggair/sdk as a dependency, pinned to 1.0.3
- An index.html with a <div id="game"> and a <div id="status"> overlay
- src/main.ts that boots a Phaser.Game at 640x480, pixelArt: true,
arcade physics, parent 'game'
- A .env holding VITE_TG_KEY, and a .gitignore that ignores .env
- Scripts: dev, build, previewDo not write any game logic yet. Just the scaffold, and confirm `npm run dev` serves a blank canvas.Good output is a blank dark canvas at localhost:5173 and absolutely nothing else. Boring, but correct.
Check one thing before you move on: that physics: { default: 'arcade' } actually made it into the config. If your agent forgot it, everything looks fine until step three, when movement explodes and you blame yourself.
Also, a gift: Vite 8 ships its bundler as a native binary, and npm has a legendary bug where it just… doesn’t install those sometimes. If npm run dev dies with "Cannot find native binding", this is your escape hatch:
npm i -D @rolldown/binding-linux-x64-gnuSwap the linux-x64 suffix for your machine (darwin-arm64 if you're on an Apple laptop). It ate ten minutes of my life.
And keep pixelArt: true. It's not a vibe setting. Without it Phaser lovingly smooths your gorgeous 32-pixel sprites into wet clay.
Step 2: make the art
Okay. This is the fun part, and also the part where I watched myself make every classic mistake in about forty minutes, so let’s speedrun my humiliation and skip you straight to the good stuff.
Rule zero: write the style rules down once, before you generate anything, and paste them into every single prompt after that. This is the style bible for the whole series:
16-bit SNES JRPG style, top-down 3/4 overworld view.
Limited 16-colour palette: forest greens, warm browns, teal, cream,
dark navy outlines. Light source from the top-left.
Crisp hard-edged pixels, no anti-aliasing, no blur, no gradients.Nothing magic about those words except that they never change, we want consistency. Five gorgeous sprites that don’t match each other are worth less than five mediocre ones that do.
The hero
Pixel art sprite of a lone fantasy courier, top-down 3/4 JRPG overworld
view, facing the camera, standing idle. 16-bit SNES JRPG style. Limited
16-colour palette: forest greens, warm browns, teal cloak, cream skin,
dark navy outlines. Clean 1-pixel dark outline around the whole character.
Light source from the top-left. The character is a single small figure
centred in the frame, wearing a teal travelling cloak and a satchel.
Crisp hard-edged pixels, no anti-aliasing, no blur, no gradients.
Solid pure magenta #FF00FF background, completely flat, no shadow on the
background. Single sprite only, no grid, no text, no labels, no border.Two bits in there are pulling serious weight.
The magenta background is a straight-up 1990s trick and I love that it still works. Image models don’t really do transparency but they are pretty good at “paint the background this exact colour”. So you pick a colour no cloak would ever be, and you delete it yourself. Chroma key. Weather forecast. Green screen. Same energy, different decade. And why not just use Inspyrenet to remove the background? I’m asking myself the same question ever since I finished this part. But anyway, it works.
“Single sprite only, no grid” exists because otherwise the model gets helpful and arranges four poses into a little contact sheet for you, roughly a third of the time. Thanks buddy, not what I asked for.
The tiles, or: how I learned to stop asking for grids
My first instinct was obviously to ask for the whole tileset in one go. One image, 4x4 grid, sixteen tiles, done, look at me, I am so efficient.
It came back beautiful. It was also completely unusable. The dirt path oozed across the cell boundaries into one big brown blob. The stone walls ran through three cells like they were paying rent in all of them. And every grass tile had a tasteful dark outline baked around it, which is a lovely artistic choice that translates to GRID LINES ALL OVER YOUR WORLD the second it tiles.
Here’s the thing I wish someone had told me: image models don’t hold a grid. They compose a picture. You asked for sixteen tiles and it heard “draw me a nice picture of some tiles”, and honestly? It did that.
So. One tile per image, full frame:
A seamless repeating pixel art grass terrain texture. Uniform short green
grass with subtle darker green tufts scattered evenly. 16-bit SNES JRPG style,
top-down overworld view. Limited 16-colour palette: forest greens, warm
browns, grey stone, teal water, dark navy. Light source from the top-left.
Crisp hard-edged pixels, no anti-aliasing, no blur, no gradients, no drop
shadows. The texture must be SEAMLESSLY TILEABLE: the left edge continues into
the right edge and the top edge continues into the bottom edge with no visible
seam. The texture fills the ENTIRE frame edge to edge. No border, no outline
around the frame, no grid lines, no text, no labels, no single centred object.Run it four times. Swap the first two sentences for dirt path, teal water, and grey stone brick wall. Change nothing from “16-bit SNES JRPG style” onwards. That unchanged block is the reason four separate generations look like they came from one artist having one consistent day.
Squashing 1024 pixels into 32 without making soup
The model hands you a 1024x1024 image. You need 32x32. How you shrink it is the difference between “bricks” and “grey static”, and I found this out the annoying way.
Do not use nearest-neighbour. It grabs one pixel out of every thousand and bins the rest, which is fine for uniform grass and absolutely lethal for a brick wall. Mine came out looking like TV snow. Average the pixels down instead, then snap the result back to a small palette. Of course, you ask your AI model to do that for you, and preprocess the assets:
Preprocess the assets in the assets/ directory using the following script: await sharp(src)
.resize(32, 32, { kernel: 'lanczos3' }) // average, do not sample
.png({ palette: true, colours: 32, dither: 0 })
.toFile(out);
The avera
